critic_raw: The design is solid. I want to probe whether the 'sentiment score' in the baseline features creates an unfair information advantage. The baseline includes 'off-the-shelf sentiment score' while the transformer 'learns representations directly from raw text.' If the sentiment scorer uses a model trained on data that includes future information (e.g., a modern pretrained sentiment model), the baseline has an information advantage from a pre-trained model, while the transformer is fine-tuned on training data only. Additionally, both models use a 'pretrained' starting point — the transformer uses BERT/DistilBERT pretrained on general corpora, while the baseline uses an 'off-the-shelf' sentiment model. This pretrained knowledge is not bounded by the training data.
defender_raw: The concern about pretrained model knowledge applies equally and symmetrically to both models. The transformer (BERT/DistilBERT) is pretrained on general text corpora. The baseline sentiment scorer is also a pretrained general model. In both cases, the pretrained knowledge is general language understanding, not SaaS support ticket domain knowledge. The fine-tuning / feature extraction occurs on the training split only. The pretrained knowledge does not leak future support ticket information. Both models benefit from general language pretraining in their respective ways — this is standard modern ML practice. The comparison tests SaaS-domain fine-tuning value, not general language knowledge.
adjudication_raw: One concern: pretrained model knowledge creating information asymmetry. Resolved: both models use pretrained general-domain knowledge symmetrically; no future data leakage. Verdict: defense_wins.
